entries.title: Button Group
entries.summary: A button group provides a navigable container of buttons.

entries.title: Buttons
entries.summary: Buttons are the primary visual indicator for a user's actions.

entries.title: Cards
entries.summary: Cards can contain a wide-variety of information like text, icons, images, Gemini responses, or actions.

entries.title: Lists
entries.summary: Lists are containers of elements with built-in scrolling and focus.

entries.title: Progress indicators
entries.summary: The progress indicator communicates the status of a known or unknown process time.

entries.title: Stack
entries.summary: A stack is a collapsed list that only displays one piece of content at a time, in a stacked visual, such as a notification or card.

entries.title: Voice indicator
entries.summary: Voice input indicator visualizes audio input from the user.

entries.title: Header
entries.summary: Headers help drivers understand which app and what part of the app they're using. They need to be brief and easy to read at a glance while on the road. Headers must include at least one of the following: text, app icon, or back button.

entries.title: UI jank detection
entries.summary: Android renders UI by generating a frame from your app and displaying it on the screen. If your app suffers from slow UI rendering, then the system is forced to skip frames. When this happens, the user perceives a recurring flicker on their screen, which is referred to as *jank*.

entries.title: Build adaptive apps for TV
entries.summary: Android TV provides a "leanback" experience where users interact with apps from a distance, typically using a remote control. Creating an adaptive app for TV requires focusing on visibility, D-pad navigation, and a large-screen-first UI design.

entries.title: Use window size classes
entries.summary: Window size classes are a set of opinionated viewport breakpoints that help you design, develop, and test responsive/adaptive layouts. The breakpoints balance layout simplicity with the flexibility of optimizing your app for unique cases.

entries.title: Build adaptive apps for XR
entries.summary: Android XR supports a variety of devices for immersive and augmented experiences. Building adaptive apps is critical for XR so users can freely resize…

entries.title: Foldables

entries.title: Stylus
entries.summary: A stylus enables artists, builders, and creators to express themselves using precise input and expansive canvases. Pressure sensitivity and low latency allow highly responsive interactions. Intuitive content management through drag and drop provides a premium usability experience.

entries.title: Tier 1 — Adaptive differentiated
entries.summary: Adaptive differentiated apps use the large screen and foldable form factors to their full potential. Differentiated apps offer a premium user experience that's productive and delightful.

entries.title: Tier 2 — Adaptive optimized
entries.summary: Optimized apps fully support all screen types and device states, including state transitions.

entries.title: Tier 3 — Adaptive ready
entries.summary: Apps that are Adaptive ready run full screen on all form factors and provide basic support for external input devices, enabling users to complete critical task flows on any device.

entries.title: Adapt Widgets for Cars
entries.summary: Widgets are coming to cars, beginning with Android Auto and followed by cars with Google built-in. Optimize your existing widget to work well on cars, making them safe, glanceable, and functional for drivers.

entries.title: Widgets
entries.summary: Widgets are customizable home screen elements that display a clear and actionable view of an app's content or actions. This content can periodically refresh.

entries.title: Canonical widget layouts
entries.summary: Craft effective widget layouts by first identifying your core content. Your layout dictates how information and interactive elements are organized within your widget. Android offers several prebuilt layouts for toolbars, text, list and grid-type widgets to streamline this process.

entries.title: Sizing
entries.summary: Design adaptable Android widgets that scale seamlessly. Use our recommended default sizes as a starting point, and test your layouts across different dimensions to ensure optimal readability and user experience.

entries.title: Jetpack Compose phases
entries.summary: Like most other UI toolkits, Compose renders a frame through several distinct *phases* . For example, the Android View system has three main phases: measure, layout, and drawing. Compose is very similar but has an important additional phase called *composition* at the start.

entries.title: App shortcuts overview
entries.summary: As a developer, you can define *shortcuts* to perform specific actions in your app. You can display these shortcuts in a supported launcher or assistant and help your users quickly start common or recommended tasks within your app.

entries.title: Add capabilities to shortcuts
entries.summary: Capabilities in `shortcuts.xml` let you declare the types of actions users can take to launch your app and directly perform a specific task. A capability consists of the name of the action and an `intent` targeting the destination in your app that resolves the user intent.

entries.title: Navigation and the back stack
entries.summary: The `NavController` holds a "back stack" that contains the destinations the user has visited. As the user navigates to screens throughout your app, the `NavController` adds and removes destinations to and from the back stack.

entries.title: Activity destinations
entries.summary: In your navigation graph, a destination can be an activity. While it is best practice to have a single activity in your app, apps often use separate activities for distinct components or screen within an app. Activity destinations can be useful in such cases.

entries.title: Nested graphs
entries.summary: Login flows, wizards, or other subflows within your app are usually best represented as nested navigation graphs. By nesting self-contained subnavigation flows in this way, the main flow of your app's UI is easier to comprehend and manage.

entries.title: Shared ViewModel Recipe
entries.summary: This recipe demonstrates how to share a `ViewModel` between different screens (entries) in Navigation 3 using a custom `NavEntryDecorator`.

entries.title: Navigate to a destination
entries.summary: The Navigation component provides a straightforward and generic way of navigating to a destination. This interface supports a range of contexts and UI frameworks. For example, you can use the Navigation component with Compose, views, fragments, activities, and even custom UI frameworks.

entries.title: Navigate with options

entries.title: API source code for Glimmer Card component

entries.title: API source code for DepthEffect
entries.summary: This documentation introduces `DepthEffect.kt`, a new file that provides the source code for simulating depth effects using shadows within the AI glasses display, enhancing the visual hierarchy of UI elements.

entries.title: API source code for DepthEffectLevels
entries.summary: This new documentation page introduces `DepthEffectLevels.kt`, which defines different levels of depth effects to simulate depth on AI glasses using shadows within Jetpack Compose Glimmer components.  It provides various levels of shadow configurations (level1 to level5) to establish a visual hierarchy.

entries.title: API source code for GoogleSansFlexTypography
entries.summary: This document provides the source code for creating a `Typography` instance using Google Sans Flex font, customized for Jetpack Compose Glimmer within the XR display AI glasses context.

entries.title: API source code for GlimmerTheme
entries.summary: This new documentation page provides the source code for the `GlimmerTheme.kt` file, which is part of the Jetpack Compose Glimmer theme, allowing visual customization of Android applications. It explains how to implement and use the Glimmer theme, including color schemes, typography, and component spacing.

entries.title: API source code for Glimmer Icon component
entries.summary: This documentation introduces the `Icon.kt` file, which defines the `Icon` composable function and related components for displaying icons with customization options such as tinting and size, and provides context for how to use icons within the Glimmer theme.

entries.title: API source code for Icon Button for Glimmer Icon Button component
entries.summary: This new documentation page introduces the `IconButton` composable function, providing source code for creating icon buttons within the Glimmer framework for Android, specifically targeting use cases in XR applications.

entries.title: API source code for Icon Sizes for Glimmer Icon component
entries.summary: This documentation introduces `IconSizes.kt`, which defines the sizes for icons within a Glimmer component.  It provides the source code for creating and customizing icon sizes in an Android application.

entries.title: API source code for Glimmer List component
entries.summary: This new file provides the source code for the `VerticalList` composable, a custom scrolling list component designed for Jetpack Compose Glimmer applications, offering features like focus-aware navigation, visual scrim edge effects, and snap behavior.

entries.title: API source code for List Item for Glimmer List component
entries.summary: This documentation introduces a new file containing the source code for the `ListItem` composable, which is used to represent individual items within a `VerticalList` in the Glimmer UI library. It provides the implementation details of the `ListItem` composable, including its different overloads (clickable and non-clickable), parameters, and usage examples, detailing its role in constructing list-based UI elements.

entries.title: API source code for List State for Glimmer List component
entries.summary: This document introduces `ListState.kt`, which provides the source code for creating a state object that controls and observes scrolling behavior within Glimmer List components in an Android environment, enabling developers to manage scroll positions and interactions.

entries.title: Material Foundation HCT TypeScript file
entries.summary: This document introduces a new file containing the source code for the HCT (Hue, Chroma, Tone) color system in TypeScript. The code provides a perceptually accurate color measurement system that can render colors accurately in different lighting environments.

entries.title: API source code for Glimmer projected context
entries.summary: This new documentation page provides the source code for `ProjectedContext.kt`, a helper class for accessing a Projected device's context and features, including launching activities and checking device connection status. It's crucial for developers building applications that interact with XR devices like AI glasses.

entries.title: API source code for Glimmer Stack component
entries.summary: This new file, `stack-source.md`, provides the source code for the `VerticalStack` composable function, which creates a vertically scrollable layout resembling a deck of cards, with items overlapping in a three-dimensional sequence.

entries.title: API source code for Stack Item Scope for Glimmer Stack component
entries.summary: This new documentation introduces `StackItemScope`, a receiver scope for managing item decorations within a `VerticalStack` composable in the Glimmer library, enabling features like depth effects and masking for individual stack items.

entries.title: API source code for Stack State for Glimmer Stack component
entries.summary: This new documentation page provides the source code for `StackState.kt`, which defines the state management for a `VerticalStack` composable in Android's Jetpack Compose for XR. This is important for handling the stack's behavior and user interactions within the XR environment.

entries.title: API source code for Surface
entries.summary: This documentation introduces the `Surface.kt` file, which defines the `surface` composable function. It provides a foundational building block for UI elements, handling aspects like clipping, borders, backgrounds, depth effects, and interaction states within the Glimmer framework.

entries.title: Implementation samples for Glimmer Title Chip component

entries.title: API source code for Glimmer Title Chip component

entries.title: Foreground services in Android 11
entries.summary: The documentation updates the URL and source metadata, and clarifies the requirements for foreground services in Android 11. Specifically, it specifies that apps targeting Android 11 or higher must declare the 'camera' and 'microphone' foreground service types when accessing the camera or microphone. It also reiterates restrictions on camera, microphone, and location access for foreground services started in the background. These changes matter because they clarify the conditions under which apps can utilize foreground services to access sensitive hardware, enhancing user privacy.

entries.title: Android Gradle plugin 9.2.0 (April 2026)
entries.summary: AGP 9.2.0 includes several R8 changes.  These changes focus on stricter handling of runtime invisible annotations to align with D8 behavior and enhance rule-based matching.  Specifically, it modifies how `-keepattributes` interacts with runtime invisible annotations and introduces support for negated member names in rules, improving control over code optimization and obfuscation.

entries.title: Android Gradle plugin 9.2.0 (April 2026)
entries.summary: AGP 9.2.0-alpha07 introduces experimental support for unified test and coverage reports. This feature generates HTML dashboards that consolidate test results and coverage data from different test types, modules, and build variants, providing a comprehensive overview. To enable this experimental feature, set `android.experimental.reportAggregationSupport=true` in your `gradle.properties` file. This change helps developers get a more complete view of their testing and coverage across an entire project, making it easier to identify areas needing attention and improve code quality.

entries.title: Layouts and navigation patterns

entries.title: Using Compose in Views
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to clarify the use of `ComposeView` within Fragments, specifically differentiating between hybrid View/Compose apps and Compose-only applications. It emphasizes the use of `ComposeView` for migration in hybrid apps while recommending a single Activity and Navigation 3 for Compose-only apps, avoiding Fragments in the latter case. This change matters because it guides developers toward the appropriate architecture based on their project's technology stack, providing clearer direction for integrating Compose UI.

entries.title: Using Views in Compose
entries.summary: The documentation for integrating Views and Fragments within Compose has been updated. The changes emphasize that `AndroidView`, `AndroidViewBinding`, and `AndroidFragment` should primarily be used as transitional tools during incremental migration to Compose. The updates encourage rewriting custom Views in Compose and adopting a Compose-only architecture for new applications. These changes aim to guide developers toward a more modern and Compose-native approach to UI development, promoting better maintainability and performance.

entries.title: Migrate Jetpack Navigation to Navigation Compose
entries.summary: The documentation clarifies that using Navigation Compose isn't mandatory in Compose-only apps, allowing continued use of Fragment-based Navigation for composable content hosting. It also emphasizes the long-term goal of replacing Fragments entirely with screen-level composables managed by Navigation Compose. Additionally, the text refines the explanation of setting up the NavController, highlighting its role in navigation and back stack management following state hoisting principles.

entries.title: Migration strategy
entries.summary: The documentation for migrating to Compose has been updated to clarify the recommended architecture for Compose-first applications. The previous version stated that using Fragments with Fragment-based navigation and hosting composable content was acceptable, but now the document recommends that the end goal is to replace Fragments with Navigation Compose.

entries.title: About subscriptions
entries.summary: Added a note clarifying the requirement for an Activity instance when displaying in-app messages. The note emphasizes that the Activity must have a valid window token and be in a state where its window is created and attached to the WindowManager. This change ensures the Play Store can correctly display the message overlay.

The documentation for the `pathPattern` attribute in the Android manifest has been significantly expanded to clarify how wildcards work and provide important usage considerations. The update explains how the dot (`.`), asterisk (`*`), and dot-asterisk (`.*`) wildcards function, including examples of what they match and do not match. Critically, the changes highlight the limitations of `pathPattern`, detailing the impact of backtracking and the behavior of greedy and lazy wildcards. It also provides best practices to avoid common pitfalls, such as avoiding patterns like `a*a` and recommending `android:pathPrefix` for prefix matching. These changes matter because they provide developers with more detailed information and guidance to correctly use `pathPattern` and avoid unexpected behavior when defining intent filters.

The documentation for `<uri-relative-filter-group>` has been updated to clarify its behavior, usage, and limitations. A critical note was added indicating that this feature is only supported on Android 15 (API level 35) and higher.  Additionally, there are minor text and formatting improvements, including the addition of `https://` prefixes for links and general clarifications throughout the examples.

It instructs users to enable the feature by setting `android.experimental.reportAggregationSupport=true` in their `gradle.properties` file. This is important because it highlights that the feature is not yet stable and might be subject to change, and also provides instructions on how to use it.

entries.title: Generic System Image releases
entries.summary: The Google Play Services version in the GSI release notes was updated from 26.02.35 to 26.11.36. This update reflects a change in the software version, which could include bug fixes, performance improvements, or new features in the Google Play Services component of the Generic System Image (GSI).

entries.title: Page from network and database
entries.summary: The documentation for v3-network-db.md has been updated to remove redundant code examples in Java and Kotlin. Additionally, the images in the document have been updated and reorganized for clarity.

entries.title: Transform data streams
entries.summary: This diff removes Java code examples and simplifies the instructions for handling separators in the UI. It also clarifies that separators are composables and suggests using composables for visual dividers.

entries.title: Page from network and database (Views)
entries.summary: This new documentation page explains how to implement paging with a network data source and a local database cache using RemoteMediator, improving the user experience by handling unreliable network connections or offline scenarios.

entries.title: Transform data streams (Views)
entries.summary: This new documentation page explains how to transform data streams when working with paged data in the Paging library, including filtering, data conversion, and adding list separators to improve UI presentation and maintain separation of concerns.

entries.title: Enable app optimization with R8
entries.summary: The documentation updates describe enhancements to Android app optimization, particularly focusing on resource shrinking and R8 compiler improvements across different Android Gradle Plugin (AGP) versions.  Key changes include enabling optimized resource shrinking for specific AGP versions, and updated details on the features and behavior of optimized resource shrinking, including its benefits and configuration, as well as clarifying which AGP versions it is enabled on.

entries.title: Add Intent filters for App Links
entries.summary: This document introduces backward compatibility considerations for Dynamic App Links on Android 14 and lower. It explains how older Android versions only consider scheme and host in manifest files, potentially leading to unintended behavior when using more specific path rules on Android 15 and higher. The document recommends adding a non-matching path to the manifest to prevent the app from handling all links on older Android versions and provides examples and warnings regarding hybrid configurations.

entries.title: Troubleshoot App Links
entries.summary: The documentation was updated to include a section on capturing bug reports for troubleshooting complex issues with App Links.  This allows developers to gather system-level error information and aid in resolving difficult-to-reproduce problems.

entries.title: Verify App Links
entries.summary: This document introduces a new section on the latency of Digital Asset Links updates, explaining how changes to the `assetlinks.json` file are reflected on end-user devices, including the impact of Android OS versions. It also provides guidance on testing updates, including considerations for re-verification and server-side caching.
